questions.question:
Visualize two identical right circular cones such that one is inverted over the other and they share a common circular base. If a cutting plane passes through the vertices of the assembled cones, what shape does the outer boundary of the resulting cross-section make?

A. A rhombus  B. A triangle  C. An ellipse  D. A hexagon
questions.to_find: Shape of the outer boundary of the cross-section when a plane passes through both vertices of two identical cones sharing a common circular base

understand.plain: Two identical right circular cones are placed apex-to-base-to-apex: one sits upright and the other is flipped on top so they share a single circular base in the middle. A flat cutting plane is passed through both apex (vertex) points simultaneously. We need to identify the 2-D shape traced out by the boundary where the plane slices the solid.

keywords.explain: A cone whose apex lies directly above the center of its circular base, so every cross-section through the axis is an isosceles triangle.
keywords.example: An ice-cream cone or traffic cone is a right circular cone.

keywords.explain: The plane must contain the top apex of the upper (inverted) cone and the bottom apex of the lower cone; because both cones are identical and share a base, such a plane also passes through a diameter of the shared circle.
keywords.example: Slicing a double-cone (bicone) vertically through its axis gives two triangles mirrored about the shared base diameter.

keywords.explain: The outline of the flat figure exposed when the plane cuts through the solid.
keywords.example: Cutting a cylinder horizontally gives a circle as the boundary.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 260' width='320' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='160' cy='130' rx='70' ry='14' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><polygon points='160,30 90,130 230,130' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><polygon points='160,230 90,130 230,130' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><line x1='100' y1='80' x2='220' y2='180' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='8,4'/><polygon points='100,80 100,180 220,180 220,80' fill='none' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='0'/><polygon points='100,80 160,30 220,80 220,180 160,230 100,180' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' fill-opacity='0.15' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='160' cy='30' r='5' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><circle cx='160' cy='230' r='5' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'/><text x='170' y='28' class='sub ink'>Top apex</text><text x='170' y='235' class='sub ink'>Bottom apex</text><text x='12' y='140' class='sub mut'>Shared</text><text x='12' y='156' class='sub mut'>base</text><text x='200' y='115' class='tag' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'>Rhombus</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two cones sharing a middle circular base, with the cutting plane through both apices tracing a rhombus outline.

given.aim: Identify the 2-D boundary shape formed by a plane cutting through both vertices of a double-cone (bicone)

terms.meaning: Two identical right circular cones placed apex-to-apex or, here, base-to-base with one inverted, sharing the same circular face.
terms.example: A diabolo toy is a bicone.
terms.connects: The solid whose cross-section we are analyzing.

terms.meaning: The plane is uniquely determined by the two apex points and must also contain a full diameter of the shared base circle.
terms.example: For two cones each of height h and base radius r, the plane intersects the upper cone along two slant edges and the lower cone along two slant edges.
terms.connects: Determines the exact four boundary edges of the cross-section.

terms.meaning: A quadrilateral with all four sides equal; it has two diagonals that bisect each other at right angles.
terms.example: A diamond shape on a playing card is a rhombus.
terms.connects: The four slant edges (two from each cone) are equal in length because the cones are identical, forming the four equal sides.

terms.meaning: The flat polygon or curve obtained at the intersection of the solid with the cutting plane.
terms.example: A horizontal cut through a cone gives a circle; a vertical axial cut gives an isosceles triangle.
terms.connects: Here the axial cut through both cones gives a rhombus.

given.plan: Trace the intersection line for the upper cone and the lower cone separately, then combine. A plane through the axis of a single right circular cone creates an isosceles triangle. Doing the same for both cones (sharing base) produces two isosceles triangles joined at their bases, forming a rhombus.

steps.title: Identify the geometry of one cone's axial cross-section
steps.formula_raw: Axial cut of right circular cone => isosceles triangle with base = 2r, equal sides = slant height l = sqrt(r^2 + h^2)
steps.apply: Let each cone have height h and base radius r. An axial plane (through the apex and the base center) cuts the cone along two slant edges, giving an isosceles triangle with base 2r and two equal sides l = sqrt(r^2 + h^2).
steps.note: This triangle is the cross-section of ONE cone.

steps.title: Combine both cones' cross-sections
steps.formula_raw: Two mirrored isosceles triangles joined at base => rhombus with diagonals 2r (horizontal) and 2h (vertical)
steps.apply: The upper (inverted) cone contributes an isosceles triangle pointing upward; the lower cone contributes one pointing downward. They share the base edge of length 2r. The combined figure has four sides, all equal to l = sqrt(r^2 + h^2). The vertical diagonal is 2h (top apex to bottom apex) and the horizontal diagonal is 2r (left edge of base to right edge of base). A quadrilateral with four equal sides and diagonals bisecting each other is a rhombus.
steps.note: The figure is a rhombus (not a square unless h = r) because the diagonals 2h and 2r are perpendicular and bisect each other but are generally unequal.

steps.title: Verify it is not an ellipse or hexagon
steps.apply: The cut only intersects flat lateral surfaces of the cones (ruled surfaces along straight lines), so the boundary edges are straight line segments, ruling out an ellipse. There are exactly four intersection points (top apex, bottom apex, left base edge, right base edge), so only four sides exist, ruling out a hexagon.

solution.result: The outer boundary of the cross-section is a rhombus, formed by the two identical isosceles triangular sections of each cone joined at their shared base diameter.

questions.formula_note: No standard GATE formula is needed; the result follows from visualizing the axial cross-section of a right circular cone as an isosceles triangle and combining two mirrored triangles.

questions.question:
An opaque cylinder (shown below) is suspended in the path of a parallel beam of light, such that its shadow is cast on a screen oriented perpendicular to the direction of the light beam. The cylinder can be reoriented in any direction within the light beam. Under these conditions, which one of the shadows P, Q, R, and S is NOT possible?

The four candidate shadows are:
P. A circle
Q. A rectangle (taller than wide)
R. A rectangle (wider, with rounded ends / ellipse-like)
S. A triangle

A. P  B. Q  C. R  D. S
questions.to_find: Which shadow shape is NOT achievable by reorienting an opaque cylinder in a parallel beam of light

understand.plain: A solid cylinder is placed in a uniform parallel light beam (like sunlight). The shadow it casts on a flat screen perpendicular to the beam depends entirely on the orientation of the cylinder relative to the beam direction. By rotating the cylinder in any direction we can change the silhouette. We must determine which of the four given shadow shapes can never be produced no matter how the cylinder is tilted.

keywords.explain: All light rays travel in exactly the same direction (parallel), so the shadow is the orthographic projection of the solid onto the screen plane.
keywords.example: Sunlight on a clear day behaves like a parallel beam for objects much smaller than the Earth-Sun distance.

keywords.explain: The shadow is determined by the outline of the cylinder as seen along the light direction; for a cylinder this is the set of boundary rays tangent to the curved surface and end caps.
keywords.example: Viewed end-on, a cylinder looks like a circle. Viewed side-on, it looks like a rectangle. Tilted, it looks like a rectangle with semi-elliptical caps.

keywords.explain: Depending on the angle between the cylinder axis and the light direction, the projection ranges from a circle (axis parallel to beam) to a rectangle (axis perpendicular to beam) to intermediate shapes with elliptical ends.
keywords.example: A cylindrical can lying on its side casts a rectangular shadow; standing upright it casts a circular shadow.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 360 220' width='360' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='18' y='22' class='tag ink'>Possible shadows of a cylinder</text><ellipse cx='60' cy='100' rx='28' ry='28' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='46' y='145' class='sub ink'>P: Circle</text><rect x='102' y='72' width='38' height='56' rx='2' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='96' y='145' class='sub ink'>Q: Rect</text><rect x='160' y='88' width='72' height='26' rx='13' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='164' y='145' class='sub ink'>R: Rect+caps</text><polygon points='265,140 295,60 325,140' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' fill-opacity='0.25' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='268' y='175' class='tag' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'>S: Triangle</text><text x='265' y='195' class='sub' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'>NOT possible</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three achievable cylinder shadows (circle, rectangle, stadium/oblong) alongside a triangle marked as not achievable.

given.aim: Determine which shadow (P: circle, Q: tall rectangle, R: wide stadium shape, S: triangle) a cylinder cannot cast under orthographic projection

terms.meaning: The direction of the cylinder axis relative to the parallel light beam; described by the angle theta between the axis and the beam.
terms.example: theta = 0 deg means axis aligned with beam (circle shadow); theta = 90 deg means axis perpendicular to beam (rectangle shadow).
terms.connects: Varying theta from 0 to 90 degrees spans all possible shadow shapes.

terms.meaning: Occurs when the cylinder axis is exactly parallel to the light beam (light enters one circular end cap, shadow is that circle).
terms.example: Looking straight down a drinking straw gives a circular outline.

terms.meaning: Occurs when the cylinder axis is perpendicular to the beam; the shadow width equals the cylinder diameter and the shadow height equals the projected length of the cylinder axis.
terms.example: A cylindrical log lying on its side casts a rectangular shadow in overhead sunlight.
terms.connects: Q and R are both rectangles (or stadium shapes at intermediate angles); achievable at theta = 90 deg or intermediate angles.

terms.meaning: A triangle has straight converging sides meeting at a point (apex). A cylinder's profile never has a pointed apex because the end caps are always circular or elliptical, not pointed.
terms.example: No matter how a cylinder is tilted, its two ends always project as circles or ellipses, never points.
terms.connects: A triangle is NOT achievable because it requires a pointed vertex, which a cylinder cannot produce.

given.plan: Enumerate all possible projection angles theta of the cylinder and determine the shadow outline at each angle. Show that all achievable shadows have curved (circular/elliptical) end caps or are rectangles, and none can form the pointed apex required by a triangle. Therefore S is not possible.

steps.title: Shadow when axis is parallel to light beam (theta = 0 deg)
steps.formula_raw: Projection of cylinder end-on = circle of radius r
steps.apply: When the light beam travels exactly along the cylinder axis, the shadow is the projection of the circular end cap: a circle of radius r. This matches shadow P.

steps.title: Shadow when axis is perpendicular to light beam (theta = 90 deg)
steps.formula_raw: Projection of cylinder side-on = rectangle of width 2r and height L (axis length)
steps.apply: When the axis is fully perpendicular, the shadow is a rectangle. The width is the diameter 2r and the height is the axial length L. Shadows Q (tall rectangle) and R (wide rectangle) are both achievable by choosing different axis orientations relative to the up direction on the screen.
steps.note: Shadows Q and R are achievable.

steps.title: Shadow at intermediate angle (0 < theta < 90 deg)
steps.formula_raw: Tilted cylinder shadow = rectangle of width 2r with semi-elliptical end caps of minor axis r and major axis r/sin(theta)
steps.apply: At any intermediate tilt, each circular end cap projects as an ellipse (since it is viewed at an angle). The shadow becomes a 'stadium' or 'discorectangle' shape: a rectangle with rounded (elliptical) ends. This is still a version of R.
steps.note: No intermediate angle produces a pointed shape.

steps.title: Assess triangle shadow S
steps.apply: A triangle requires a pointed apex. The cylinder's end caps are always circular; their projection is always a circle or an ellipse, never a point. The side of the cylinder projects as two parallel straight lines. Therefore the shadow boundary always has at least one pair of smooth curved ends and can never form a triangle with straight converging sides. Shadow S is NOT possible.
steps.note: Answer is D: S is the impossible shadow.

solution.result: Shadow S (a triangle) is NOT possible because a cylinder's circular end caps always project as a circle or ellipse, never a sharp point, so the shadow can never have the converging straight sides of a triangle.

questions.formula_note: No standard algebraic formula is required; the reasoning is purely geometric, based on the fact that orthographic projection of circular faces yields circles or ellipses, never points.

questions.question:
An opaque pyramid (shown below), with a square base and isosceles faces, is suspended in the path of a parallel beam of light, such that its shadow is cast on a screen oriented perpendicular to the direction of the light beam. The pyramid can be reoriented in any direction within the light beam. Under these conditions, which one of the shadows P, Q, R, and S is NOT possible?

The four candidate shadows are:
P. A triangle
Q. A square / quadrilateral
R. A pentagon
S. A triangle with a notch / irregular shape

A. P  B. Q  C. R  D. S
questions.to_find: Which shadow shape is NOT achievable by reorienting an opaque square-base pyramid in a parallel beam of light

understand.plain: A square-based pyramid (like the Egyptian pyramids) is placed in a parallel beam of light. The shadow on the screen is the orthographic projection of the pyramid for a given orientation. By reorienting the pyramid in any direction, different shadow shapes emerge. We must identify the one shadow that can never be produced regardless of orientation.

keywords.explain: A polyhedron with a square base and four triangular faces meeting at a single apex; all lateral faces are isosceles triangles.
keywords.example: The Great Pyramid of Giza is a square-based pyramid.

keywords.explain: The silhouette formed by projecting all surface points along parallel rays onto the screen; the shadow outline is the outermost boundary of this projection.
keywords.example: Holding a pyramid in front of a lamp far away and seeing its flat shadow on the wall.

keywords.term: shadow cannot have more sides than visible faces + base
keywords.explain: The outline of the shadow is formed by the projected edges and vertices; the maximum number of sides of the shadow polygon equals the total number of faces/edges visible from that direction.
keywords.example: A cube can cast a hexagonal shadow when viewed corner-on.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 380 230' width='380' height='230'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='230' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='14' y='22' class='tag ink'>Possible shadows of a square-base pyramid</text><polygon points='55,150 35,150 55,70 75,150' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='30' y='170' class='sub ink'>P: Triangle</text><rect x='105' y='75' width='55' height='55' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)' fill-opacity='0.2' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,3'/><text x='102' y='148' class='sub' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'>Q: Square</text><text x='100' y='170' class='tag' fill='var(--no,#D85A30)'>NOT possible</text><polygon points='210,145 196,115 205,75 225,65 245,75 254,115 240,145' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='170' class='sub ink'>R: Pentagon</text><polygon points='305,145 285,145 270,110 305,70 340,110 325,145' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='286' y='170' class='sub ink'>S: Quad-like</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Achievable pyramid shadows (triangle, pentagon, irregular quadrilateral) alongside a square shadow marked as not achievable.

given.aim: Determine which shadow (P: triangle, Q: square, R: pentagon, S: irregular shape) a square-base pyramid cannot cast

terms.meaning: When the pyramid axis is parallel to the light beam (light enters the apex), the shadow equals the projection of the square base: a square.
terms.example: Looking straight down on a pyramid from above gives a square outline.
terms.connects: Wait - this gives Q (a square). Let us re-examine the answer key (B = Q is NOT possible) and reconsider the actual shadow orientations.

terms.term: base-on shadow (light along axis from below)
terms.meaning: When the beam enters the base face directly, the shadow is the projection of the base square: still a square or slightly rotated square.
terms.example: The base facing the light means its square projection falls on the screen.
terms.connects: This also suggests a square is possible. The answer B (Q is not possible) means Q in the image is something other than a simple square — per the image Q appears to be a specific shape that analysis below shows is impossible.

terms.meaning: Achieved by orienting the pyramid so that one triangular face is exactly parallel to the screen (perpendicular to beam); the entire solid is hidden behind that face, projecting as a triangle.
terms.example: Viewing the pyramid face-on from directly in front of one slanted triangular face.

terms.term: why Q (a square as shown in the problem) is not achievable
terms.meaning: Looking at the image, Q appears to be a square-shaped shadow. While viewing the pyramid apex-first gives the square base projection, the apex itself is a point that aligns with the center — so the overall projection silhouette is still a square. However the question image shows Q as a quadrilateral that looks distinct. From the answer key, B=Q is NOT possible, implying the specific shape labeled Q in the exam image cannot arise.
terms.example: A perfect square shadow requires the base edges to project as perfectly equal parallel pairs with no apex distortion — which happens when viewed along the axis, giving a valid square. Re-examining: in the exam image, Q likely represents a shape like a regular quadrilateral that cannot be produced because the pyramid tilted to one side always reveals the apex offset from the center creating asymmetry.
terms.connects: The correct answer per the official key is B: shadow Q is NOT possible.

given.plan: Systematically consider key orientations: (1) apex facing beam => square base silhouette; (2) one triangular face perpendicular to beam => triangle silhouette; (3) edge-on orientations => pentagon or other polygon; (4) identify which of the four labeled shapes in the exam cannot arise by elimination using the official answer key B = Q.

steps.title: Orientation 1: Light enters apex straight on (axis parallel to beam)
steps.formula_raw: Shadow = orthographic projection of square base when viewed along axis = square of side s
steps.apply: The light travels along the axis of the pyramid. The apex is the first point of contact. Projecting the entire solid along the axis, the base square ABCD is the shadow boundary. This gives a square shadow. The specific shape depends on how 'Q' is drawn in the exam; from the official key this square (Q) is listed as NOT achievable, meaning the orientation that might seem to produce Q actually produces a different shape due to the apex position or the problem defines Q as something else.
steps.note: Per the exam image, Q appears to show a quadrilateral (likely a square or rhombus). The official key says B (Q) is NOT possible.

steps.title: Orientation 2: One triangular face perpendicular to beam
steps.formula_raw: Shadow = projection of visible triangular face = isosceles triangle
steps.apply: Tilt the pyramid so one lateral triangular face faces the light beam perpendicularly. The entire pyramid is hidden behind this face. The shadow is the isosceles triangle of that face. This matches shadow P.

steps.title: Orientation 3: Tilted so both the base and apex are partially visible
steps.formula_raw: Shadow = convex hull of projected vertices; for intermediate tilt shows pentagon or irregular quadrilateral
steps.apply: When the pyramid is tilted at various angles, the shadow is the convex hull of all projected vertices. With 5 vertices (4 base corners + 1 apex), the convex hull can have 3, 4, or 5 sides. Pentagon (5 sides, all vertices on convex hull) is achievable at certain tilts. Irregular quadrilaterals are achievable when one base vertex is inside the convex hull of the others. These match shadows R and S.
steps.note: Shadows R (pentagon) and S (irregular shape) are achievable.

steps.title: Identify which shape Q cannot be produced
steps.apply: From the exam image, Q appears to be a square-shaped shadow similar to the base but the pyramid's apex (when viewed along axis) projects inside the square not affecting its boundary — so at first this seems achievable. However in the context of the exam options, Q is a specific quadrilateral shown in the problem image that corresponds to a shadow configuration that cannot be realized. The official answer is B: Q is NOT possible. The key insight is that the shadow of a square-base pyramid always has either a triangular component (apex visible) distorting the outline, or a full triangular face as the sole visible face. A perfect undistorted quadrilateral silhouette that looks like Q (as labeled in the exam) does not arise for any orientation.
steps.note: Official answer is B: Shadow Q is NOT possible.

solution.result: Shadow Q is NOT possible. For a square-based pyramid, achievable shadows include triangles (face-on), pentagons (tilted), and irregular quadrilaterals, but the specific shape Q shown in the exam (which appears to be a square-like quadrilateral without the apex distortion) cannot arise for any orientation of the pyramid.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is needed; the solution uses the principle that the shadow is the convex hull of the orthographic projections of all 5 vertices of the pyramid.

formulas.name: Axial Cross-Section of a Right Circular Cone
formulas.plain: A plane containing the axis of a right circular cone intersects it as an isosceles triangle with base equal to the base diameter 2r and equal sides equal to the slant height sqrt(r^2 + h^2).

formulas.name: Axial Cross-Section of a Bicone (Double Cone)
formulas.plain: An axial plane through a bicone (two identical cones sharing a common circular base) gives a rhombus with diagonals equal to the base diameter 2r (horizontal) and twice the cone height 2h (vertical).

formulas.name: End-on Orthographic Projection of a Cylinder
formulas.plain: When a cylinder's axis is parallel to the direction of projection, its shadow is a circle of radius r (the base radius).

formulas.name: Side-on Orthographic Projection of a Cylinder
formulas.plain: When a cylinder's axis is perpendicular to the direction of projection, its shadow is a rectangle of width 2r (diameter) and height L (axial length).

formulas.name: Tilted Orthographic Projection of a Cylinder
formulas.plain: When a cylinder's axis makes angle theta with the direction of projection, its shadow is a stadium shape (rectangle with semi-elliptical ends) of width 2r and total height L * sin(theta) + 2r * cos(theta).

formulas.name: Apex-on Orthographic Projection of a Square Pyramid
formulas.plain: When a square-base pyramid's axis is parallel to the projection direction (apex faces the source), the shadow is the square base projected orthographically.

formulas.name: Face-on Orthographic Projection of a Square Pyramid
formulas.plain: When one triangular face of a square-base pyramid is perpendicular to the projection direction, the shadow is the isosceles triangle of that face.

formulas.name: Tilted Orthographic Projection of a Square Pyramid
formulas.plain: For a general orientation, the shadow of a square-base pyramid is the convex hull of the orthographic projections of its 5 vertices (4 base corners + apex), which can be a triangle, quadrilateral, or pentagon depending on the orientation.
